case_name: Balancing Li and cobalt removal from lake water for potable reuse under strict limits and low-energy preferences
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for Li- and cobalt-impacted lake water intended for potable reuse while balancing strict finished-water limits against low-energy and chemical-minimization constraints.

input_data.scenario_background: A utility is developing an indirect potable reuse scheme that blends advanced-treated municipal effluent with impacted lake water receiving runoff and episodic discharges from an upstream battery-material handling area. Monitoring shows dissolved Li is persistently elevated while cobalt occurs at lower concentration but must still be tightly controlled for potable reuse. Several technically viable routes exist, including high-rejection membranes and precipitation or selective ion exchange, but the utility prefers lower-energy and lower-chemical options if they can still meet the strict finished-water targets reliably. The trade-off is that the most robust Li removal options tend to generate concentrate or require higher operating complexity, while lower-energy options can be less selective or more sensitive to competing ions and seasonal water-quality changes.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water for potable reuse should reliably achieve very low dissolved metal concentrations, with cobalt reduced to near-trace levels and Li reduced to a stringent site-specific target suitable for blending into a potable supply.
secondary_goals: limit bulk chemical addition and sludge production
maintain stable compliance during seasonal lake-water quality changes
avoid treatment trains that create difficult residual streams unless justified by compliance need

engineering_constraints.residuals_management: prefer low-sludge and low-concentrate generation; residuals requiring hazardous or off-site management are a disadvantage
engineering_constraints.robustness_requirement: must tolerate seasonal turbidity and DOC variation while maintaining tight effluent reliability for potable reuse

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: reduce turbidity and natural organic matter to protect downstream selective treatment
core_unit_functions.dual_media_filtration: provide clarified low-solids feed and improve robustness
core_unit_functions.weak_acid_cation_exchange_for_cobalt_con: capture divalent cobalt and reduce load to the membrane stage
core_unit_functions.low_pressure_reverse_osmosis_for_li_poli: provide the dependable dissolved Li reduction needed for strict potable-reuse targets
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust permeate chemistry and corrosion stability
core_unit_functions.advanced_disinfection: provide final microbial barrier for potable reuse

applicability_rationale: Li is difficult to remove to very low levels with conventional precipitation or standard softening alone, so a membrane polishing barrier is valuable for compliance reliability
pre-removal of cobalt by ion exchange reduces divalent metal load and can allow lower-pressure membrane operation than a membrane-only route
front-end solids and organics control is important for lake water with moderate DOC and seasonal turbidity variation
this hybrid route balances the strict effluent limit with somewhat lower energy and lower concentrate strength than a full high-recovery RO train treating all contaminants at once
potential_risks: still produces membrane concentrate that requires management
ion exchange performance for cobalt can be affected by calcium and magnesium competition
seasonal increases in DOC or algae can raise fouling risk if pretreatment is not well controlled

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: remove particulate matter and reduce organics before resin contact
core_unit_functions.dual_media_filtration: protect ion-exchange vessels from solids carryover
core_unit_functions.selective_cation_exchange: remove cobalt effectively and provide partial to potentially substantial Li removal depending on resin chemistry and operating strategy
core_unit_functions.advanced_disinfection: provide final microbial barrier for potable reuse

applicability_rationale: this route aligns strongly with low-energy and chemical-minimization goals because it avoids high-pressure membranes and bulk precipitation chemicals
cation-exchange approaches are well suited for cobalt because cobalt is divalent and generally more strongly exchanged than Li
a selective resin route can be attractive where residual management of spent regenerant is easier than membrane concentrate management

it ranks below the top option because Li removal to a strict site-specific limit is less universally reliable and can depend heavily on resin selectivity, background cations, and regeneration strategy
potential_risks: Li selectivity may be insufficient for very stringent targets in the presence of sodium, calcium, and magnesium
regenerant handling can conflict with chemical-minimization goals if frequent regeneration is required
breakthrough monitoring must be tight to avoid compliance excursions

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: remove suspended solids and some organic matter
core_unit_functions.dual_media_filtration: provide stable feed for downstream chemical treatment
core_unit_functions.high_pH_precipitation_softening: promote precipitation of cobalt hydroxide and associated metal solids
core_unit_functions.recarbonation: return pH and alkalinity toward potable-reuse-compatible conditions
core_unit_functions.microfiltration: remove precipitated solids and polish treated water
core_unit_functions.advanced_disinfection: provide final microbial barrier for potable reuse

applicability_rationale: cobalt can be removed effectively by pH-driven precipitation because it is a divalent metal that forms insoluble hydroxide species at elevated pH
the process is familiar and can be robust for cobalt under variable feed concentrations
this route ranks below ion exchange because Li remains highly soluble and is not reliably reduced to a strict low target by precipitation alone
it also conflicts with chemical-minimization objectives due to lime or caustic demand and sludge generation
potential_risks: Li removal is likely inadequate for stringent potable-reuse targets
produces metal-bearing sludge requiring careful dewatering and disposal
tight pH control is needed to avoid residual dissolved cobalt or scaling

core_unit_functions.enhanced_coagulation: reduce DOC and colloids before membrane treatment
core_unit_functions.ultrafiltration: provide strong particulate and algae barrier for membrane protection
core_unit_functions.reverse_osmosis: simultaneously reject Li, cobalt, and other dissolved constituents
core_unit_functions.stabilization: condition low-mineral permeate for distribution and blending
core_unit_functions.advanced_disinfection: provide final microbial barrier for potable reuse

applicability_rationale: this is the most technically direct route for achieving strict dissolved-contaminant limits because RO offers broad-spectrum rejection
it is especially attractive if the potable-reuse program also needs additional control of salts or trace organics beyond Li and cobalt
despite strong technical capability, it ranks lower because the case prioritizes low energy and chemical minimization
full membrane treatment imposes higher energy use, more complex operation, and a larger concentrate-management burden than hybrid or resin-first alternatives
potential_risks: concentrate disposal may be costly or difficult
membrane fouling risk increases with lake-water DOC, algae, and seasonal solids spikes
post-treatment stabilization is mandatory for low-mineral permeate

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: reduce suspended solids and some organic matter
core_unit_functions.manganese_oxide_or_adsorptive_media: adsorb or co-remove some transition-metal species including cobalt under favorable conditions
core_unit_functions.advanced_oxidation_process: oxidize trace organics but not provide primary Li removal
core_unit_functions.filtration: polish particulate matter and protect final disinfection
core_unit_functions.advanced_disinfection: provide final microbial barrier for potable reuse

applicability_rationale: included as a technically plausible but misaligned route because adsorptive media may help with cobalt control in some waters
the route does not provide a dependable primary mechanism for Li removal, which is the harder compliance driver in this case
advanced oxidation can add value for potable reuse organics control, but it does not solve the dissolved Li challenge
this option ranks last because it adds complexity and energy without addressing the controlling contaminant as effectively as the higher-ranked alternatives
potential_risks: insufficient Li removal for strict finished-water targets
media exhaustion and replacement timing may be uncertain for mixed-cation loading
AOP may increase operational complexity and chemical use without proportional benefit for the target metals

preferred_rank_pattern: a hybrid route combining conventional lake-water pretreatment with targeted cobalt removal and a Li polishing barrier should rank near the top
selective ion exchange should appear as a strong low-energy alternative but not necessarily first when Li limits are very strict
full RO should be recognized as highly capable yet downgraded by energy and concentrate constraints
precipitation-based cobalt control should appear as plausible but limited by poor Li removal

must_reflect: strict potable-reuse effluent reliability for both contaminants
preference for lower-energy treatment over blanket membrane use when possible
importance of minimizing bulk chemical addition and sludge production
trade-off between membrane concentrate handling and regenerant or sludge management

must_contain: why Li is harder to remove than cobalt with conventional precipitation or adsorption
why cobalt can often be handled by divalent-metal-focused ion exchange or precipitation
why lake-water turbidity and DOC justify pretreatment before resin or membrane steps
why technically strong membrane options can rank below hybrid options under low-energy and chemical-minimization constraints

should_mention: Li breakthrough or insufficient selectivity in ion exchange systems
concentrate management burden for RO-based options
sludge generation from cobalt precipitation routes
seasonal fouling and water-quality variability in lake-water treatment
